plainMeaning: Reverence to the awakened ones, who give us the Dharma, teach it, lead by it, steer us along it like a charioteer, and reign over it like supreme world-emperors.

wordByWord.gloss: to the givers of the Dharma
wordByWord.etymology: dhamma (< Skt. dharma) + dāya/dāyaka 'giver' (√dā 'to give'); gen./dat. pl. -āṇaṁ governed by the namo of namotthuṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: The Arihanta does not hoard the teaching but bestows it; the -daya stem here continues the 'giver' motif of the preceding verse (abhaya-, cakkhu-…dayāṇaṁ).

wordByWord.gloss: to the teachers (proclaimers) of the Dharma
wordByWord.etymology: dhamma + desaya (< Skt. deśaka 'one who points out, expounds', √diś 'to indicate'); gen./dat. pl.
wordByWord.notes: deśanā = the Tīrthaṅkara's sermon delivered in the samavasaraṇa; hence 'expounder', not merely 'giver'.

wordByWord.gloss: to the leaders of the Dharma
wordByWord.etymology: dhamma + nāyaga (< Skt. nāyaka 'leader, chief', √nī 'to lead'); gen./dat. pl.
wordByWord.notes: nāyaka implies the foremost authority who heads the fourfold saṅgha founded upon the Dharma.

wordByWord.gloss: to the charioteers of the Dharma
wordByWord.etymology: dhamma + sārahi (< Skt. sārathi 'charioteer'); gen./dat. pl. -īṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: A sārathi both drives and reins; the Jina steers practitioners along the path and curbs them from straying — the active counterpart to nāyaka's leadership.

wordByWord.etymology: dhamma + vara 'best, choicest, supreme' (Skt. vara); qualifier opening the long compound dhammavara-cāuraṁta-cakkavaṭṭīṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Printed with a space, but syntactically the head of the final compound — the monarchy is one of the 'supreme Dharma', not of land.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. catur-anta 'having four extremities/quarters' — the four directions a cakravartin conquers.
wordByWord.notes: Transposed to the spiritual register: the four directions become the totality over which the Dharma-sovereign holds sway.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. cakravartin 'one who turns the wheel (cakra)' of empire; gen./dat. pl. -īṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Here a metaphor: the Jina turns the wheel of Dharma (dharma-cakra), not of armies — see glossary 'caturanta-cakravartin'.

literalTranslation: (Reverence) to the givers of Dharma, to the proclaimers of Dharma, to the leaders of Dharma, to the charioteers of Dharma, to the four-quartered universal monarchs of the supreme Dharma.
idiomaticTranslation: Homage to them who bestow the Dharma and preach it, who lead by it and drive us along it like a skilled charioteer, who reign as the wheel-turning emperors of the supreme Dharma over all four quarters.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This clause forms the 'dhamma-' cluster, five titles bound by anaphora on dhamma. They are ordered as a deliberate intensification of agency. First dhamma-daya: the Jina gives the Dharma — the gentlest verb, simply handing it over. Then dhamma-desaya: he expounds it, the public deśanā of the samavasaraṇa. Then dhamma-nāyaga: he is its head and authority, the one others follow. Then dhamma-sārahi: more than leading from the front, he actively drives and reins the disciple, the charioteer who both propels toward liberation and restrains from wrong turns. The series culminates in the long compound dhammavara-cāuraṁta-cakkavaṭṭi: the Jina is the cakravartin of the supreme Dharma. Where a worldly emperor conquers the four quarters of the earth by force and the seven jewels, the Jina turns the dharma-cakra and reigns over the whole moral cosmos by truth alone — a pointed contrast the listener is meant to feel. The single particle of homage (namo, carried over from namotthuṇaṁ) governs all five datives, so each title is an act of bowing, not mere description.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the distinctively Jain insight that a Tīrthaṅkara is venerated not for power held but for liberation enabled: he gives, teaches, leads, and steers — the soteriology is transitive. The sārathi image anticipates the reflexive-transitive couplets that follow (tiṇṇa-tāraya, buddha-bohaya): the Jina is one who, having mastered the path himself, conveys others across. The cakravartin metaphor reframes sovereignty: supreme authority belongs to dharma-cakra-pravartana (turning the wheel of Dharma), the act by which a Tīrthaṅkara establishes the tīrtha, not to digvijaya (territorial conquest).
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited, the five titles let the practitioner relate to the Jina in ascending intimacy — first as a giver to receive from, finally as a charioteer to entrust one's course to. The reciter examines: do I merely receive the Dharma, or let it lead and rein me? The cakravartin image redirects ambition for worldly dominion toward dominion over one's own conduct.

crossReferences.text: Aupapātika (Uvavāiya) Sūtra — the Namutthuṇaṁ formula of homage to the Arihantas
crossReferences.source: Śvetāmbara Ardhamāgadhī canon (ed. Leumann; tr. tradition)

crossReferences.text: Bhagavatī & Jñātādharmakathā — recurrence of the Śakra-stava clauses
Preceding verse 'abhayadayāṇaṁ … bohidayāṇaṁ' (the five 'givers'), continuing the -daya motif into dhamma-daya

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit–English Dictionary (1899), Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon
sources.consultedFor: Roots and senses of dāyaka (√dā), deśaka (√diś), nāyaka (√nī), sārathi, vara, catur-anta, and cakravartin.

sources.title: Tirthankara — overview of the Dharma-preaching ford-maker
sources.consultedFor: Confirmation of the Tīrthaṅkara as supreme preacher (deśaka) and founder/leader of the tīrtha, grounding dhamma-desaya and dhamma-nāyaga.

sources.title: Chakravarti (universal monarch) — four-quarter conquest concept
sources.consultedFor: The cāuraṁta-cakkavaṭṭi (caturanta-cakravartin) imagery — the wheel-turner ruling the four quarters, here transposed to the dharma-cakra.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim Gujarati-script verse text and its placement as clause 6 of the Śakra-stava.

notes: The five titles are all genitive/dative plurals (-āṇaṁ/-īṇaṁ) governed by the namo of namotthuṇaṁ, hence rendered as 'to the…'. 'ધમ્મવર' is printed with a following space but is the head qualifier of the single compound dhammavara-cāuraṁta-cakkavaṭṭīṇaṁ ('universal monarchs of the supreme Dharma'); the word-internal spacing is preserved per the book's orthography and is not a separate independent word. One online auto-gloss erroneously reads dhamma-sārahi as 'without a charioteer' (conflating it with Uttarādhyayana imagery); the correct reading is dharma-sārathi, 'charioteer of the Dharma'. Token segmentation follows the spaced printed units to ensure 100% coverage.
